arrays.html.principles:
• Arrays are objects in Java. They have a fixed length once created.
  • Array indices are 0-based. The last element is always at length - 1.
  • Use Arrays utility class (e.g., Arrays.sort(), Arrays.copyOf()) for common operations.
arrays.html.misunderstandings:
• Trying to resize an array directly. You must create a new array and copy elements.
  • Confusing array length (a property) with String length() (a method).
  • Forgetting that object arrays initialize with nulls, not empty objects.
arrays.html.checklist:
• You can iterate over an array using both standard and enhanced for-loops.
  • You know how to use the Arrays utility class.
  • You can explain how a 2D array is actually an "array of arrays" in Java.
arrays.html.interview:
Question 1
What happens if you access an invalid array index?
Java throws an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException at runtime. It does not return undefined or allow arbitrary access like some languages.
Question 2
How is a 2D array structured in memory?
A 2D array in Java is an array of references to other arrays. This means the sub-arrays can be of different lengths (jagged arrays).

build-tools.html.principles:
• Build tools (Maven, Gradle) standardize the project structure and build lifecycle.
  • Dependency management prevents "JAR hell" by automatically downloading libraries and their transitive dependencies.
  • Use the wrapper (mvnw, gradlew) so anyone can build the project without pre-installing the tool.
build-tools.html.misunderstandings:
• Committing target/ or build/ directories to Git. (They should be in .gitignore).
  • Modifying downloaded JARs directly instead of updating the pom.xml or build.gradle.
  • Thinking you only need a build tool for large projects. Even small projects benefit from standardized builds and dependency management.
build-tools.html.checklist:
• You can explain the difference between Maven and Gradle.
  • You know how to add a dependency to a pom.xml or build.gradle.
  • You can run a clean and build command from the terminal.
build-tools.html.interview:
Question 1
What is a Transitive Dependency?
When your project depends on Library A, and Library A depends on Library B, Library B is a transitive dependency of your project. Maven/Gradle manage this automatically.
Question 2
Maven vs Gradle?
Maven uses XML (pom.xml) and focuses on conventions. Gradle uses a Groovy/Kotlin DSL, is highly customizable, and uses incremental builds which makes it much faster for large projects (hence Google chose it for Android).

classes.html.principles:
• A class is a blueprint. Objects are instances built from that blueprint.
  • Encapsulate internal state by making fields private and exposing behavior via public methods.
  • Constructors initialize the state of a new object. They have no return type.
classes.html.misunderstandings:
• Using public fields instead of getters/setters, violating encapsulation.
  • Creating 'God Classes' that try to do everything, instead of splitting responsibilities.
  • Forgetting to define a no-argument constructor if frameworks (like Spring/Hibernate) require it.
classes.html.checklist:
• You can explain the difference between a class and an object.
  • You know how the 'this' keyword works and when it is required.
  • You can create an immutable class.
classes.html.interview:
Question 1
What is a Constructor?
A special block of code called when an object is instantiated. It shares the class name, has no return type, and initializes the object's state.
Question 2
What is 'this' keyword?
It is a reference to the current object. It is used to resolve ambiguity between instance variables and local parameters, or to call another constructor.

collections-deep.html.principles:
• Choose the right data structure for the job (e.g., ArrayList for fast reads, HashSet for uniqueness).
  • Understand time complexity (Big O notation) of basic operations like add, contains, and remove.
  • Use the Map interface for key-value pairs; it does not inherit from Collection.
collections-deep.html.misunderstandings:
• Using LinkedList thinking it is faster than ArrayList. For modern hardware caching, ArrayList is almost always faster.
  • Forgetting to override equals() and hashCode() when using custom objects as keys in a HashMap or elements in a HashSet.
  • Modifying a collection during iteration using a normal for-each loop.
collections-deep.html.checklist:
• You can explain the internal working mechanism of a HashMap.
  • You know the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators.
  • You can correctly override equals() and hashCode() according to the contract.
collections-deep.html.interview:
Question 1
How does a HashMap work internally?
It uses an array of Nodes. It computes the hash code of the key to find the array index. If there's a collision, it uses a linked list (or Red-Black tree in Java 8+) at that index.
Question 2
ArrayList vs LinkedList?
ArrayList uses a dynamic array (fast random access O(1), slow insertions/deletions in middle O(n)). LinkedList uses a doubly-linked list (slow random access O(n), fast insertions/deletions O(1) if node is known).

concurrency.html.principles:
• Concurrency is about managing shared state. Synchronization is the primary tool to prevent race conditions.
  • Modern Java prefers higher-level abstractions like ExecutorService and CompletableFuture over raw Threads.
  • Deadlocks happen when two or more threads wait for each other to release locks forever.
concurrency.html.misunderstandings:
• Thinking 'volatile' makes operations atomic. It only guarantees visibility of changes across threads.
  • Calling Thread.run() instead of Thread.start(). (run() executes synchronously in the current thread).
  • Using outdated classes like Vector or Hashtable instead of ConcurrentHashMap or Collections.synchronizedList.
concurrency.html.checklist:
• You can explain the thread lifecycle states (New, Runnable, Blocked, Waiting, Terminated).
  • You know the difference between wait()/notify() and Thread.sleep().
  • You can implement a Thread-safe Singleton.
concurrency.html.interview:
Question 1
Runnable vs Callable?
Runnable's run() method returns void and cannot throw checked exceptions. Callable's call() method returns a Future result and can throw checked exceptions.
Question 2
What does the 'synchronized' keyword do?
It acquires an intrinsic lock on an object, ensuring that only one thread can execute a block of code on that object at a time.

controlflow.html.principles:
• Keep loops and conditionals simple. Extract complex logic into well-named methods.
  • Use the enhanced for-loop whenever you don't need the index.
  • Switch statements (especially Java 14+ switch expressions) are cleaner than long if-else chains.
controlflow.html.misunderstandings:
• Forgetting the 'break' statement in a traditional switch, causing unintended fall-through.
  • Using a while loop when a for loop is more appropriate (e.g., when you know the number of iterations).
  • Modifying a collection while iterating over it with a standard for-each loop (causes ConcurrentModificationException).
controlflow.html.checklist:
• You can refactor a long if-else chain into a modern switch expression.
  • You know when to use a do-while loop vs a while loop.
  • You understand how to safely break out of nested loops using labels.
controlflow.html.interview:
Question 1
Can you use a String in a switch statement?
Yes, since Java 7, Strings are supported in switch statements. Under the hood, it uses the String's hash code.
Question 2
What is the 'continue' keyword?
It skips the rest of the current loop iteration and proceeds immediately to the next iteration.

datatypes.html.principles:
• Primitives live on the stack (fast), Objects live on the heap (overhead).
  • Floating point arithmetic is not precise. Use BigDecimal for financial calculations.
  • Understand implicit casting (widening) vs explicit casting (narrowing).
datatypes.html.misunderstandings:
• Assuming == compares values for wrapper classes. It compares references. Use .equals().
  • Using float or double for money. They suffer from rounding errors.
  • Forgetting that char is essentially a 16-bit integer (UTF-16 code unit) in Java.
datatypes.html.checklist:
• You know the exact memory size of int, long, double, and boolean.
  • You can predict overflow behavior when adding two large ints.
  • You know when to use Integer vs int.
datatypes.html.interview:
Question 1
Primitive vs Wrapper Class?
Primitives (int, double) are raw values stored on the Stack (fast, memory efficient). Wrapper classes (Integer, Double) are full objects stored on the Heap and can be used in Collections.
Question 2
What is Autoboxing?
It is the automatic conversion that the Java compiler makes between the primitive types and their corresponding object wrapper classes (e.g., converting int to Integer).
Question 3
Why can't 'var' be used for class fields?
Because var relies on type inference from the right side of the equals sign at the moment of declaration. Class fields have default values and their types need to be known by the compiler before any code runs.

datetime.html.principles:
• Always use the java.time package (introduced in Java 8) instead of java.util.Date.
  • java.time classes are immutable and thread-safe by design.
  • Understand the difference between human time (LocalDate) and machine time (Instant).
datetime.html.misunderstandings:
• Using Date or Calendar in new code. They are flawed, mutable, and legacy.
  • Forgetting time zones. LocalDateTime does NOT store a time zone. Use ZonedDateTime if the zone matters.
  • Modifying a LocalDate and forgetting to reassign it (e.g., calling date.plusDays(1) but not capturing the return value).
datetime.html.checklist:
• You can create, format, and parse a LocalDate.
  • You know the difference between Duration and Period.
  • You can convert between a legacy Date object and an Instant.
datetime.html.interview:
Question 1
Why was java.time introduced?
Legacy Date/Calendar classes were mutable (not thread-safe), had poor API design (months were 0-indexed), and didn't clearly separate machine time from human time.
Question 2
LocalDateTime vs ZonedDateTime?
LocalDateTime is just a clock and calendar on a wall; it has no concept of where in the world it is. ZonedDateTime includes a specific time zone rule set.

design-patterns.html.principles:
• Patterns are proven solutions to common architectural problems, not rigid rules.
  • Favor Composition over Inheritance (Strategy pattern, Decorator pattern).
  • Keep code loosely coupled and highly cohesive.
design-patterns.html.misunderstandings:
• Trying to force a pattern where a simple function would do. Over-engineering is a trap.
  • Implementing the Singleton pattern incorrectly (e.g., without considering multi-threading). Enum is the safest way.
  • Confusing Factory Method (creating one object) with Abstract Factory (creating families of objects).
design-patterns.html.checklist:
• You can implement a thread-safe Singleton.
  • You understand the Builder pattern and when to use it over telescoping constructors.
  • You can explain the Strategy pattern and how it relates to dependency injection.
design-patterns.html.interview:
Question 1
What is the Singleton Pattern?
A creational pattern that ensures a class has only one instance, and provides a global point of access to it.
Question 2
What is Dependency Injection?
A technique where an object receives other objects that it depends on (dependencies), rather than creating them itself. It makes code highly testable and loosely coupled.

enums-records.html.principles:
• Enums provide type safety for a fixed set of constants (e.g., DAYS_OF_WEEK).
  • Records (Java 14+) reduce boilerplate for immutable data carriers.
  • Enums can have fields, constructors, and methods, just like regular classes.
enums-records.html.misunderstandings:
• Using String constants (public static final String RED = "RED") instead of Enums, losing type safety.
  • Trying to instantiate an Enum using the 'new' keyword. (The JVM handles instantiation).
  • Assuming Records are just "less typing" for Java Beans. Records are strictly immutable; they don't have setters.
enums-records.html.checklist:
• You can create an Enum with a custom constructor and properties.
  • You understand the 'values()' and 'valueOf()' methods of Enums.
  • You know how to define a Record and access its components.
enums-records.html.interview:
Question 1
Are Enums thread-safe?
Yes, the JVM guarantees that Enums are instantiated only once. This makes them a perfect and simple way to implement the Singleton design pattern.
Question 2
Can a Record implement an Interface?
Yes. Records cannot extend other classes (they implicitly extend java.lang.Record), but they can implement interfaces.

exceptions-io.html.principles:
• Only catch exceptions you can handle. Otherwise, throw them up the stack.
  • Checked exceptions force you to deal with recoverable errors (like network/file issues).
  • Always use try-with-resources (Java 7+) to automatically close streams and prevent memory leaks.
exceptions-io.html.misunderstandings:
• Catching an exception and doing nothing (swallowing it). This makes debugging impossible.
  • Using exceptions for standard control flow (e.g., throwing an exception instead of returning false).
  • Confusing final (keyword), finally (block), and finalize() (method).
exceptions-io.html.checklist:
• You can explain Checked vs Unchecked exceptions.
  • You can write a proper try-catch-finally block.
  • You know how to read/write a file using modern NIO APIs (Files and Paths).
exceptions-io.html.interview:
Question 1
Error vs Exception?
Errors (like OutOfMemoryError) are serious JVM-level issues you shouldn't try to catch. Exceptions are conditions that a reasonable application might want to catch.
Question 2
If a finally block throws an exception, what happens?
If a finally block throws an exception, it suppresses any exception that was thrown in the try or catch blocks, making debugging very confusing.

generics.html.principles:
• Generics provide compile-time type safety for collections and APIs.
  • Understand Type Erasure: Generics exist at compile time but are erased at runtime.
  • Use bounded wildcards (? extends T, ? super T) for flexible API design (PECS principle).
generics.html.misunderstandings:
• Thinking List<String> is a subclass of List<Object>. It is not.
  • Trying to instantiate a generic type parameter (new T()). This fails because of type erasure.
  • Confusing upper bounds (? extends) with lower bounds (? super).
generics.html.checklist:
• You can write a generic class and a generic method.
  • You understand the Producer Extends, Consumer Super (PECS) mnemonic.
  • You can explain why arrays and generics don't mix well.
generics.html.interview:
Question 1
What is Type Erasure?
To maintain backward compatibility with older Java versions, the compiler removes all generic type information during compilation, replacing parameters with Object or their bounds.
Question 2
What is the PECS Principle?
Producer Extends, Consumer Super. Use '? extends T' when you only read from a collection. Use '? super T' when you only write to a collection.

inheritance.html.principles:
• Inheritance models an "IS-A" relationship. A Dog IS-A Animal.
  • Prefer Composition over Inheritance when the relationship is "HAS-A".
  • The 'super' keyword accesses the immediate parent class's methods and constructors.
inheritance.html.misunderstandings:
• Using inheritance just to reuse code (code reuse is better achieved via composition).
  • Forgetting that private members of a parent class are NOT accessible in the child class directly.
  • Misunderstanding method hiding (for static methods) vs method overriding (for instance methods).
inheritance.html.checklist:
• You can explain polymorphism and how method overriding enables it.
  • You know the role of the Object class as the root of all Java classes.
  • You can explain what 'protected' access means.
inheritance.html.interview:
Question 1
Does Java support multiple inheritance?
Java does not support multiple inheritance for classes to avoid the "Diamond Problem". However, a class can implement multiple interfaces.
Question 2
What is Polymorphism?
The ability of a single interface or reference to take on multiple forms. In Java, this usually means a parent reference variable can point to any child object.

interfaces.html.principles:
• Interfaces define a contract. They specify WHAT a class should do, not HOW.
  • Program to an interface, not an implementation, for flexible architecture.
  • Java 8+ allows default and static methods in interfaces, breaking the "no implementation" rule for better API evolution.
interfaces.html.misunderstandings:
• Confusing Abstract Classes with Interfaces. Abstract classes can have state (instance variables); interfaces generally do not.
  • Thinking you must implement every method of an interface if you declare your class abstract. (You don't).
  • Overusing interfaces when a simple class or function would suffice.
interfaces.html.checklist:
• You can explain the difference between an Interface and an Abstract Class.
  • You know when and why to use 'default' methods.
  • You can explain multiple inheritance of type via interfaces.
interfaces.html.interview:
Question 1
What is a Marker Interface?
An interface with no methods or fields (e.g., Serializable, Cloneable). It is used to tag a class to signal special behavior to the JVM or a framework.
Question 2
Interface vs Abstract Class?
A class can implement multiple interfaces but extend only one abstract class. Interfaces cannot have state (instance variables), while abstract classes can.

io-network.html.principles:
• Streams handle raw bytes (InputStream/OutputStream); Readers/Writers handle characters.
  • Always wrap streams in Buffered streams to minimize expensive native I/O calls.
  • Use modern java.nio.file (Files, Paths) over legacy java.io.File.
io-network.html.misunderstandings:
• Forgetting to close streams, causing file locks and memory leaks. (Always use try-with-resources).
  • Assuming character encoding is consistent. Always specify the Charset (e.g., StandardCharsets.UTF_8).
  • Loading entire giant files into memory at once instead of processing them line-by-line or in chunks.
io-network.html.checklist:
• You can read a text file line-by-line using Files.lines().
  • You know the difference between an InputStream and a Reader.
  • You can make a basic HTTP request using the modern HttpClient.
io-network.html.interview:
Question 1
java.io vs java.nio?
java.io uses blocking, stream-oriented I/O. java.nio (New I/O) uses buffers and channels, and supports non-blocking (asynchronous) I/O, which is highly scalable.
Question 2
What is Serialization?
The process of converting a Java object's state into a byte stream so it can be saved to a file or sent over a network. The class must implement Serializable.

java-project-structure.html.principles:
• Follow standard directory layouts (e.g., src/main/java, src/test/java). Don't invent your own.
  • Organize packages by feature (com.app.user, com.app.order) rather than by layer (com.app.controllers, com.app.services).
  • Keep configuration files (like application.properties) in src/main/resources.
java-project-structure.html.misunderstandings:
• Putting all classes in the default (unnamed) package. This makes them invisible to classes in named packages.
  • Mixing test code and production code in the same directory.
  • Not using a unique package name (like a reversed domain name: com.yourcompany.project).
java-project-structure.html.checklist:
• You can set up a standard Maven/Gradle project structure from scratch.
  • You understand the difference between the src/ and target/ (or build/) directories.
  • You know where to place static assets and property files.
java-project-structure.html.interview:
Question 1
Why do we use reversed domain names for packages?
To prevent naming collisions. If two different companies create a 'User' class, com.apple.User and com.microsoft.User can coexist perfectly in the same application.
Question 2
What goes in src/main/resources?
Non-Java files needed at runtime, such as configuration properties, XML files, templates, and static images. They are bundled into the root of the resulting JAR.

jdbc.html.principles:
• JDBC is the foundation; ORMs (like Hibernate) sit on top of it.
  • Always use PreparedStatement instead of Statement to prevent SQL injection.
  • Manage transactions explicitly (Connection.setAutoCommit(false)) when executing multiple related updates.
jdbc.html.misunderstandings:
• Concatenating strings to build SQL queries. This is a massive security vulnerability (SQL Injection).
  • Not closing the ResultSet, Statement, and Connection in a finally block or try-with-resources.
  • Executing N+1 queries by fetching IDs and then looping to fetch details, instead of using a JOIN.
jdbc.html.checklist:
• You can establish a connection to a database using DriverManager.
  • You can execute a SELECT query and iterate through a ResultSet.
  • You can execute an INSERT/UPDATE using a PreparedStatement.
jdbc.html.interview:
Question 1
Statement vs PreparedStatement?
PreparedStatement is pre-compiled by the database, making it faster for repeated executions. More importantly, it safely handles parameters, preventing SQL Injection.
Question 2
What is Connection Pooling?
Creating a database connection is expensive. A pool maintains a cache of active connections that can be reused by multiple requests, significantly improving performance.

jvm-internals.html.principles:
• The JVM acts as an abstraction layer between Java bytecode and the underlying operating system.
  • Garbage Collection is automatic memory management. It reclaims heap space by destroying unreachable objects.
  • The JIT compiler profiles running code and optimizes "hot" methods by converting them to native machine code.
jvm-internals.html.misunderstandings:
• Believing Garbage Collection prevents all memory leaks. (Holding onto references unintentionally still causes leaks).
  • Assuming System.gc() forces garbage collection immediately. It is only a suggestion to the JVM.
  • Confusing the Heap (for objects) with the Stack (for method execution and local primitives).
jvm-internals.html.checklist:
• You can explain the difference between the Stack, Heap, and Metaspace.
  • You understand the Generational Garbage Collection hypothesis (Young vs Old generation).
  • You know what a ClassLoader does.
jvm-internals.html.interview:
Question 1
Stack vs Heap?
**Stack** stores local variables and method calls (fast, automatic cleanup, limited size). **Heap** stores all objects (slower, managed by Garbage Collector, shared across threads).
Question 2
How does Garbage Collection work?
The GC automatically identifies and deletes objects that are no longer referenced by any part of the program. It uses the "Mark and Sweep" algorithm and divides the Heap into generations based on object lifetime.
Question 3
What is a Memory Leak in Java?
It happens when objects are no longer needed but are still referenced by static variables or active collections, preventing the Garbage Collector from freeing up their memory.

lambdas.html.principles:
• Lambdas provide a concise way to implement Single Abstract Method (SAM) interfaces.
  • They enable functional programming patterns, passing behavior as arguments.
  • Lambdas do not create a new scope; variables from the enclosing scope must be effectively final.
lambdas.html.misunderstandings:
• Thinking lambdas are just syntactic sugar for anonymous inner classes (they use invokedynamic at runtime).
  • Trying to mutate a local variable from the enclosing scope inside a lambda.
  • Overusing lambdas when a simple method reference (Class::method) is cleaner.
lambdas.html.checklist:
• You can convert an anonymous inner class into a lambda expression.
  • You know the core functional interfaces: Predicate, Function, Supplier, Consumer.
  • You understand what 'effectively final' means.
lambdas.html.interview:
Question 1
What is a Functional Interface?
An interface with exactly one abstract method. It can have multiple default or static methods, but only one abstract method to match the lambda signature.
Question 2
Lambda vs Anonymous Inner Class?
Lambdas use 'invokedynamic' instruction, avoiding the generation of a new .class file per lambda. Also, 'this' inside a lambda refers to the enclosing class, not the lambda itself.

math-utils.html.principles:
• Math class methods are static; you don't instantiate it.
  • For cryptographic or secure random numbers, always use SecureRandom, not Math.random() or java.util.Random.
  • Use BigDecimal for exact decimal representation, especially for currency.
math-utils.html.misunderstandings:
• Using double/float for money. Floating-point arithmetic is inherently imprecise (e.g., 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004).
  • Using Math.random() in a high-concurrency loop (it synchronizes on a single seed, causing contention). Use ThreadLocalRandom instead.
  • Creating a new Random object inside a loop.
math-utils.html.checklist:
• You can correctly use BigDecimal for a currency calculation.
  • You know how to generate a random integer within a specific range.
  • You understand the difference between Math.floor(), Math.ceil(), and Math.round().
math-utils.html.interview:
Question 1
Why not use double for currency?
Because double uses base-2 fractions, it cannot represent base-10 fractions (like 0.1) exactly, leading to rounding errors. BigDecimal solves this.
Question 2
ThreadLocalRandom vs Random?
ThreadLocalRandom is isolated to the current thread, avoiding the contention overhead that java.util.Random has in multi-threaded environments.

methods.html.principles:
• A method should do one thing and do it well (Single Responsibility Principle).
  • Keep parameter lists short. If a method takes more than 3-4 arguments, consider passing an object.
  • Understand method signatures: name and parameter types. Return type is NOT part of the signature.
methods.html.misunderstandings:
• Confusing method overloading (same name, different parameters) with overriding (subclass replacing parent's method).
  • Thinking you can pass primitives by reference. Java is strictly pass-by-value.
  • Ignoring the return value of immutable object methods (e.g., calling str.toLowerCase() without assigning the result).
methods.html.checklist:
• You can explain method overloading and write an example.
  • You know how varargs (... syntax) works under the hood (it's an array).
  • You can differentiate between static and instance methods.
methods.html.interview:
Question 1
Overloading vs Overriding?
Overloading is compile-time polymorphism (same name, different parameters). Overriding is runtime polymorphism (subclass provides specific implementation of a parent's method).
Question 2
Can you overload a method based only on return type?
No. The compiler uses the method signature (name + parameters) to distinguish methods. Return type is ignored during overload resolution.

modern-features.html.principles:
• Java has evolved rapidly since Java 8. Keep up with LTS releases (11, 17, 21).
  • Features like Records, switch expressions, and pattern matching drastically reduce boilerplate.
  • Project Loom (Virtual Threads) changes the paradigm of concurrent programming in Java.
modern-features.html.misunderstandings:
• Thinking 'var' makes Java dynamically typed. It does not; types are inferred at compile-time.
  • Using a Record for a class that needs mutable state (Records are strictly immutable).
  • Thinking Text Blocks change how strings are stored in memory (they are just syntactic sugar for multiline string literals).
modern-features.html.checklist:
• You can refactor an old POJO into a Record.
  • You can use modern Switch Expressions yielding a value.
  • You understand the basic concept of Virtual Threads (Java 21).
modern-features.html.interview:
Question 1
What is the 'var' keyword?
Introduced in Java 10, 'var' allows local variable type inference. The compiler infers the specific type from the right-hand side of the assignment.
Question 2
What are Java Records?
Introduced in Java 14, Records are transparent carriers for immutable data. They automatically generate constructors, accessors, equals(), hashCode(), and toString().

operators.html.principles:
• Short-circuit operators (&&, ||) prevent unnecessary evaluation and NullPointerExceptions.
  • The ternary operator is for simple expressions, not complex logic branches.
  • Bitwise operators act directly on binary representations and are extremely fast.
operators.html.misunderstandings:
• Confusing = (assignment) with == (equality).
  • Using == to compare Strings. Strings are objects, so use .equals().
  • Not understanding operator precedence, leading to unexpected math results.
operators.html.checklist:
• You can explain why (a != null && a.length() > 0) is safe.
  • You can write a bitwise shift operation to multiply by 2.
  • You know the difference between prefix (++i) and postfix (i++).
operators.html.interview:
Question 1
Difference between & and && ?
&& is short-circuiting; if the first condition is false, the second isn't evaluated. & evaluates both sides always (also acts as bitwise AND).
Question 2
Why doesn't == work for String comparison?
== compares memory addresses (reference equality). Two strings with identical text might be stored in different memory locations.

reflection.html.principles:
• Reflection allows inspecting and modifying code at runtime (e.g., reading private fields).
  • Use it sparingly. It breaks encapsulation and is slower than direct code execution.
  • Frameworks (Spring, Hibernate, JUnit) rely heavily on reflection to work their magic (dependency injection, mapping, testing).
reflection.html.misunderstandings:
• Using reflection to bypass security restrictions in normal application logic.
  • Thinking reflection is fast. It involves dynamic resolution, preventing JIT optimizations.
  • Forgetting that reflection makes code brittle to refactoring (e.g., renaming a field breaks reflection code looking for it by name).
reflection.html.checklist:
• You can get the Class object of a type.
  • You can read a private field's value using reflection.
  • You understand how annotations and reflection work together to power frameworks.
reflection.html.interview:
Question 1
What is Reflection?
An API that allows a Java program to examine or modify the runtime behavior of applications running in the JVM. It can inspect classes, interfaces, fields, and methods at runtime.
Question 2
Why is Reflection slow?
Because it involves types that are dynamically resolved, certain JVM optimizations cannot be performed. It also involves type checking and security checks at runtime instead of compile-time.

setup.html.principles:
• Environment variables (PATH, JAVA_HOME) are how your OS finds Java.
  • Always match your IDE's JDK version with your project's target version to avoid compile errors.
  • Understand the command line before relying entirely on IDE 'Run' buttons.
setup.html.misunderstandings:
• Thinking you need to download a JRE. Modern Java (11+) only distributes the JDK.
  • Having multiple JDKs installed and not knowing which one the terminal is using.
  • Confusing IDE errors with actual Java compiler errors.
setup.html.checklist:
• You can set JAVA_HOME and verify it via the terminal.
  • You can compile a .java file manually using javac.
  • You can run the resulting .class file using java.
setup.html.interview:
Question 1
What is the Classpath?
The classpath tells the JVM and compiler where to look for user-defined classes and packages.
Question 2
What happens if you run 'java MyClass.class'?
It throws an error (ClassNotFoundException). The java command expects a fully qualified class name without the .class extension.

streams.html.principles:
• Streams are pipelines for processing sequences of elements. They don't store data.
  • Stream operations are lazy: intermediate operations don't execute until a terminal operation is called.
  • Streams can easily be parallelized, but it's only beneficial for large datasets or CPU-intensive tasks.
streams.html.misunderstandings:
• Trying to reuse a Stream. A stream cannot be operated on or reused after a terminal operation has been called.
  • Modifying the underlying collection while processing a stream (causes unpredictable results or exceptions).
  • Assuming parallelStream() is always faster. Thread overhead can make it slower for small collections.
streams.html.checklist:
• You can distinguish between intermediate (e.g., map, filter) and terminal operations (e.g., collect, forEach).
  • You know how to use Collectors to group or partition data.
  • You understand the difference between map() and flatMap().
streams.html.interview:
Question 1
Intermediate vs Terminal operations?
Intermediate operations (filter, map) return a new stream and are lazy. Terminal operations (collect, count) return a non-stream result and trigger execution.
Question 2
What is flatMap()?
It transforms each element of a stream into a stream of new elements, and then flattens these inner streams into a single output stream.

strings.html.principles:
• Strings are strictly immutable. Once created, they cannot be changed.
  • Understand the String Pool. Literal strings are cached and reused to save memory.
  • Use StringBuilder for string manipulation in loops to avoid memory bloat.
strings.html.misunderstandings:
• Using == instead of .equals() for string comparison. This is the #1 beginner mistake.
  • Thinking String methods like .trim() or .replace() modify the original string. They return a NEW string.
  • Concatenating strings inside a loop using the '+' operator, which creates many hidden objects.
strings.html.checklist:
• You can explain why Strings are immutable and the security/performance benefits.
  • You know the difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer.
  • You can write a regex to split or validate a complex string.
strings.html.interview:
Question 1
What is the String Pool?
A special storage area in the Java Heap. When a string literal is created, the JVM checks the pool. If it exists, it returns the reference, saving memory.
Question 2
StringBuilder vs StringBuffer?
Both are mutable sequences of characters. StringBuffer is thread-safe (synchronized), whereas StringBuilder is not, making StringBuilder faster for single-threaded use.

testing.html.principles:
• Write tests that verify behavior, not implementation details.
  • Follow the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern for clear, readable tests.
  • Use mocking frameworks (like Mockito) only to isolate the unit being tested from external dependencies (DBs, APIs).
testing.html.misunderstandings:
• Aiming for 100% code coverage without caring about test quality (testing getters/setters).
  • Writing tests that pass/fail depending on the order they are run (lack of test isolation).
  • Mocking everything, resulting in tests that just mirror the code and don't verify real logic.
testing.html.checklist:
• You can write a basic JUnit 5 test with @Test, @BeforeEach, and assertions.
  • You know how to mock a dependency using Mockito.
  • You understand the difference between Unit, Integration, and End-to-End tests.
testing.html.interview:
Question 1
What is a Mock?
A fake object used in testing that simulates the behavior of a real object in controlled ways. It allows you to test a class in isolation without hitting a real database or network.
Question 2
Unit vs Integration Testing?
Unit tests verify a single class or method in isolation. Integration tests verify that multiple components (e.g., your code + the database) work correctly together.

what-is-java.html.principles:
• Java is a platform (JVM), not just a language. Understand the bytecode lifecycle.
  • Backward compatibility is king. Java prioritizes stability over breaking changes.
  • Strong typing prevents runtime errors. Trust the compiler.
  • Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA) relies on the abstraction of the JVM over the OS.
what-is-java.html.misunderstandings:
• Thinking Java is interpreted like Python. It is compiled to bytecode, then JIT-compiled to machine code.
  • Confusing the JRE (Runtime) with the JDK (Development Kit).
  • Believing Java is too slow. Modern JVMs are heavily optimized and can rival C++ in long-running processes.
  • Ignoring memory management because of Garbage Collection (memory leaks still happen!).
what-is-java.html.checklist:
• You can explain the difference between JDK, JRE, and JVM.
  • You understand the compilation and execution phases (javac vs java).
  • You can explain what bytecode is and why it's useful.
  • You know the role of the Garbage Collector at a high level.
what-is-java.html.interview:
Question 1
Is Java Pass-by-Value or Pass-by-Reference?
Java is strictly Pass-by-Value. When passing object references, the value of the reference (the pointer) is passed by value.
Question 2
What is the JIT Compiler?
The Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler is part of the JVM. It compiles frequently executed bytecode ("hot spots") into native machine code at runtime for performance.
